# Introduction

This project investigates the movement behavior of two individuals. A tracking of their movement was conducted using different tracking methods, one using GPS-based app (Christopher) and one using Google Timeline @GoogleTimeline (Amelia).
Descriptive spatio-temporal trajectory analysis are applied to compare the mobility behavior. The analysis focuses on movement trajectories as sequences of timestamped geographic coordinates and further spatial analysis will include distance calculations, home-based radius analysis, and spatial overlay with OpenStreetMap @OpenStreetMap road network data to conclude frequently used road types. The interpretation of the results are supported by the participants’ own knowledge of their daily movements.

The following research questions get analysed:

* Which road types are most frequently used based on the recorded trajectories?
* How far is the average or maximal movement radius from their home when they travel?
* How far do they travel on average and at what speed?
* How long are the individuals out of their home on average?
* How long is the commuting time from home to ZHAW?

As there are differences in data structure, sampling intervals, GPS accuracy and preprocessing effort in both datasets, an additional objective is to evaluate how different tracking methods influence the quality and reliability of trajectories, movement analysis and processability.
